planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Lazy Housewife is an 1810 German heirloom pole bean that is one of the oldest and most historically significant American heirloom beans, documented in German seed catalogs since the early 1800s and named for the ease of shelling — the pods are stringless and the beans pop out with minimal effort.

directions: Direct sow 1–1.5 inches deep after last frost in fertile, well-drained soil at the base of a trellis, teepee poles, or corn stalks; provide 6 feet of vertical support for the vigorous vines. Lazy Housewife is one of America's most documented heirloom pole beans, appearing in American seed catalogs since at least 1810 and recorded by seed historian William Woys Weaver as a bean brought to America by German immigrants; the name refers to the ease of preparation — the pods are stringless when harvested young as snap beans, and the mature white seeds shell out of the dried pods with minimal effort, hence 'lazy housewife.' The pods are 5–7 inches long, round, and stringless, with plump white seeds marked with a distinctive black eye at maturity. Equally excellent as a snap bean (harvest young), shell bean (harvest green pods when seeds have filled), or dry bean; the cooked flavor of the mature seeds is rich, creamy, and outstanding.
sources: Weaver, William Woys. Heirloom Vegetable Gardening

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil near a support structure; Romano is the Italian flat-podded snap bean standard in Italian and Mediterranean cooking, prized for its wider, flatter pods, tender texture when cooked, and rich, nutty flavor that is distinctly superior to standard round snap beans for braising and sautéing.

directions: Direct sow 1–1.5 inches deep in warm soil after last frost; provide a 5–6 foot trellis or teepee for the vines and begin harvesting when pods are flat and 4–5 inches long, before they string. Romano is the classic Italian flat-podded or 'Italian flat bean,' a pole type with wide, flat, 5–7 inch pods that have a different texture and flavor profile from standard round snap beans when cooked — more tender, less fibrous, with a rich, earthy, slightly nutty flavor that makes them the preferred snap bean for Italian sautés (fagioli in padella), long-braised dishes with olive oil and garlic, and agrodolce preparations. The flat pods become stringy if left to mature too long; harvest when pods are still flat and not yet showing bean bulges. A bush Romano type is also available. Highly productive in warm climates; excellent for Gulf Coast spring and fall bean production.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Snowcap is a compact bush bean with strikingly beautiful seeds — white with maroon-red markings concentrated around the hilum — and outstanding flavor as both a shell bean and dried bean, with a creamy texture and rich taste that earned it wide popularity among heirloom bean enthusiasts.

directions: Direct sow 1–1.5 inches deep after last frost in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Snowcap is a bush-type bean that needs no support and can be direct-seeded in rows 18 inches apart. Snowcap is an heirloom bush dry bean producing attractive, round-to-oval seeds that are white with a distinctive cap of maroon-red coloring concentrated around the seed scar (hilum) — the 'snowcap' pattern from which it takes its name. The flavor is rich, buttery, and creamy when cooked, with a thin skin that doesn't need pre-soaking for most applications; it is outstanding in soups, chilis, and bean salads where its firm-yet-creamy texture holds up well. An attractive plant for the vegetable garden with modest height and compact habit; the ornamental seeds are a bonus when drying for seed saving. One of the most beautiful and best-flavored heirloom dry beans in the Seed Savers Exchange collection.

common_name: Eye of the Goat Bean (Ojo de Cabra)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, well-drained soil; Eye of the Goat (Ojo de Cabra) is a traditional Mexican and Southwestern heirloom bean with striking beige seeds marked by a tan-ringed dark eye — one of the most beautiful and flavor-rich heirloom beans in the Southwestern tradition.

directions: Direct sow 1–1.5 inches deep after last frost in warm, well-drained to dry soil; Eye of the Goat is adapted to dry conditions and does well with minimal supplemental irrigation in zones 7–11 once established. Eye of the Goat (Ojo de Cabra) is a traditional Mexican heirloom dry bean with large, oval, cream-to-beige seeds marked with a distinct tan 'eye' or ring surrounding a dark hilum — resembling, as the name suggests, the eye of a goat. This bean has been grown in Mexico and the American Southwest for generations and is particularly heat- and drought-tolerant, making it valuable in hot, dry climates. When cooked, the seeds have a rich, earthy, slightly sweet flavor with a creamy texture that holds its shape well; excellent in soups, stews, and as refried beans in traditional Mexican cooking. An outstanding heirloom for Gulf Coast and Southwest gardeners who need a reliable dry bean in hot, occasionally dry summers.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in fertile, well-drained soil; Soldier bean is a New England heirloom dry bean with large, white seeds marked with a distinctive red figure near the hilum that resembles a standing soldier or musketeer — one of the most visually striking and historically significant New England heirloom beans.

directions: Direct sow 1.5 inches deep after last frost in fertile, well-drained soil; soldier bean is a bush type that performs best in cool to moderate summer climates (zones 3–7) but is grown across a wide range. Soldier bean is a New England heirloom dry bean with a long history in Maine and New England, documented in American seed catalogs for well over a century; the seeds are large, kidney-shaped, and pure white with a striking red figure near the hilum that — with some imagination — resembles a soldier or musketeer with a distinctive hat and cloak, making it one of the most visually interesting of all heirloom beans. The cooked flavor is mild, creamy, and rich, similar to navy beans but larger; soldier beans are the traditional bean of New England baked beans, Boston baked beans, and bean soups and make excellent succotash. An outstanding heirloom for seed saving, showing true and breeding reliably from year to year.
